pageSubline: Statistical analysis of 3.05M Mexican government procurement contracts

evolution.title: Model Evolution
evolution.subtitle: From expert-weighted checklist (v3.3) to per-sector ML with cross-model validation and explainability (v5.2 layer).

steps.v33Desc: 8 expert-weighted factors, IMF CRI aligned. AUC barely above random.
steps.v40Desc: Z-score normalization, Bayesian calibration, 9 corruption cases, 12 features.
steps.v50Desc: 12 sector sub-models, 15 documented cases, temporal train/test split.
steps.v51Desc: 22 cases including SAT EFOS Case 22: 38 RFC-confirmed ghost companies.
steps.v60Desc: ~748 cases, ~603 vendors. Vendor-stratified split (no vendor in both train+test), time-windowed labels, Optuna TPE.
steps.v52Desc: SHAP per-vendor explanations (456K vendors) + PyOD cross-model ensemble (9.3M scores) + KS drift monitoring. See Vendor Profile → Risk tab, Contract detail → AI Confirmed, Dashboard → Cross-Model.
steps.v85Desc: 1,417 GT cases · 861 vendors. ElasticNet (C=0.2243, l1_ratio=0.7545), 18 active features, c_pu=0.32. HR=11.01% OECD-compliant. Run: CAL-v8-202605020212.

overview.p1End: with 95% confidence intervals. The v0.8.5 model uses vendor-stratified validation (no vendor appears in both training and test sets), producing an honest test AUC of 0.785. A score of 0.35 means this contract's procurement characteristics closely resemble those from documented corruption cases.
overview.formulaDescStart: Where z = z-score features, beta = learned coefficients, sigma = logistic sigmoid, c = PU correction (0.32). S(z) is a
overview.formulaDescEnd: — it measures how closely a contract's procurement characteristics resemble those from documented corruption cases. It is not a literal probability of corruption.
overview.highRiskSummaryEnd: (critical + high). Thresholds calibrated against 1,417 documented cases to yield a rate consistent with international procurement risk ranges. v0.8.5 is the active model (May 2, 2026), using vendor-stratified validation.
overview.tableFootnote: * 3,051,294 scored contracts of 3,058,286 total. The remaining ~7K contracts lack sector assignment and were excluded from scoring.

features.p1: Each contract is described by 18 z-score features normalized by sector and year baselines. v0.8.5 adds cobid_herfindahl, recency_z, amount_residual_z, amendment_flag, and pub_delay_z to the v5.x behavioral set. The chart below shows the learned ElasticNet coefficients (C=0.2243, l1_ratio=0.7545). Positive coefficients increase the risk score; negative coefficients decrease it.
features.footerNote: Coefficients from Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.2243, l1_ratio=0.7545, 200 trials). ElasticNet mix balances sparsity and stability. Six features regularized to exactly zero (sameDayContracts, winRate, singleBid, sectorSpread, coBidRate, priceHypConfidence). Run: CAL-v8-202605020212.

riskEvidence.p1: Each risk factor is grounded in peer-reviewed literature on procurement corruption. The table below shows each factor's learned coefficient, global evidence strength, and key source. Hover over a factor name for mechanism details.

findings.finding1Title: Price Volatility is the strongest predictor (+0.558)
findings.finding1Body: Vendors with wildly varying contract sizes remain the strongest corruption signal (+0.558). This is followed by price ratio (+0.358), vendor concentration (+0.327), and co-bid herfindahl (+0.272). Institution diversity is protective (-0.388). Recency is also protective (-0.247): recently active vendors are less suspicious.
findings.finding2Title: Key sign changes from v5.1 to v0.8.5
findings.finding2Item1End: — Win rate collapsed from a strong predictor to near-zero. Many GT vendors receive direct awards rather than winning competitive bids, making win_rate a confounded signal.
findings.finding2Item2End: — In v5.1, cross-sector operation was protective. In v0.8.5, large GT vendors like Edenred and Toka operate across sectors, causing the model to associate sector spread with corruption. This is a labeling artifact.
findings.finding2Item3End: — Single bidding provides no discriminating power in v0.8.5. Known-bad vendors tend to win through direct awards rather than single-bid competitive procedures, so this classic OECD indicator has no signal in Mexican federal procurement ground truth.
findings.finding3BodyEnd: — The co_bid_rate coefficient was regularized to exactly 0.0 by L2 regularization. Co-bidding patterns do not help distinguish known-bad contracts from random contracts in this dataset. This suggests the known-bad vendors operate through market concentration rather than collusion.
findings.finding4Strong: These findings challenge the standard OECD/IMF anti-corruption framework
findings.finding4End: for Mexican procurement. The traditional indicators (single bidding, direct awards, short ad periods) that dominate international methodology are weak or reversed in our ground truth data. This may reflect the specific characteristics of Mexican procurement corruption, or the limitations of our ground truth sample.

validation.p1Start: The model was trained and validated against
validation.p1End: , matching 861 vendors to ~302K contracts across all 12 sectors in the COMPRANET database.
validation.footnote: *Case 9 (PEMEX Emilio Lozoya) shares vendors with the Odebrecht case. Documented for reference but does not contribute additional training data. The table above shows the original 22 foundation cases from v5.1. v0.8.5 expanded the ground truth to 1,417 cases and 861 vendors through systematic COMPRANET investigation, ASF audit cross-referencing, and SAT EFOS integration.
validation.technicalNote: Train AUC 0.797 / Test AUC 0.785 — vendor-stratified 70/30 split, no vendor appears in both train and test sets. HR=11.01% OECD-compliant (within 2–15% benchmark). Model v0.8.5 active since May 2, 2026. Run ID: CAL-v8-202605020212.

methods.zScoreP1: Each feature is normalized relative to its sector and year baseline. A direct award in Defensa (where 80% are direct) is less suspicious than in Educacion (where 50% are direct).
methods.zScoreNote: 3,372 baselines computed across 12 sectors, ~24 years, and 16 features. Fallback hierarchy: sector+year (if n >= 30), sector-only (if n >= 100), global.
methods.mahalanobisP1: Captures multivariate anomalies — contracts that are unusual across multiple dimensions simultaneously. This replaces the 5 hardcoded interaction pairs from v3.3 with a full covariance-based approach.
methods.mahalanobisNote: Covariance estimated with Ledoit-Wolf shrinkage for stability in small sectors. P-values computed against chi-squared distribution with 16 degrees of freedom.
methods.logisticP1: Optuna-tuned ElasticNet (C=0.2243, l1_ratio=0.7545, 200 trials) trained on ~302K known-bad contracts from 1,417 corruption cases with vendor-stratified 70/30 split (no vendor in both train and test). 12 per-sector sub-models + 1 global fallback capture sector-specific corruption patterns.
methods.logisticNote: Train AUC: 0.797, Test AUC: 0.785. ElasticNet mix drives 6 uninformative features to exactly zero while preserving more features than near-pure L1, improving robustness over v0.6.5.
methods.puP1Start: Since unlabeled contracts are not necessarily clean (some may be corrupt but undetected), we apply a Positive-Unlabeled learning correction:
methods.puNote: v0.8.5 estimated c = 0.32 via Elkan & Noto holdout on 13 models (1 global + 12 per-sector). This reflects the heterogeneity of the ground truth (861 vendors with diverse patterns). With c=0.32, scores are rescaled by ~3.1x and capped at 1.0. IMPORTANT: The SCAR assumption is structurally violated — known cases are high-profile scandals, not a random sample of all corruption.
methods.bootstrapP1: Each contract receives a 95% confidence interval from 500 bootstrap resamples of the training data. A score of 0.35 [0.22, 0.48] means: the risk indicator is 0.35, but given data uncertainty, it could be as low as 0.22 or as high as 0.48.

v52layer.p1End: adds three independent ML tools on top of the active v0.8.5 risk scores. These tools do not replace or alter any contract risk scores — they provide additional explanations and cross-validation signals.
v52layer.shapBody: Exact Shapley values (φᵢ = βᵢ × (zᵢ − E[zᵢ])) computed for each vendor using their sector-specific model coefficients. Shows which of the 16 z-score features drove the risk score up or down.
v52layer.shapWhere: Vendor Profile → Risk tab → SHAP Analysis panel
v52layer.pyodBody: Isolation Forest + COPOD ensemble anomaly detection run independently on all 3.05M contracts. A high ensemble score means the contract is a statistical outlier even without knowing the ground truth label.
v52layer.pyodWhere: Contract detail → AI Confirmed badge · Dashboard → Cross-Model Validation · Explore → AI Confirmed filter
v52layer.driftBody: Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests compare the 2024–2025 feature distribution against the 2002–2020 training baseline. Detects when new procurement data develops patterns unlike the training period.
v52layer.aiConfirmedEnd: when the supervised v0.8.5 logistic model scores it as high or critical risk and the unsupervised PyOD ensemble independently scores it above the 0.50 anomaly threshold. Two completely different methods — one trained on labeled corruption cases, one trained on no labels at all — agree that this contract is unusual. This convergence reduces the false-positive rate for investigation triage.
v52layer.noteStart: Note: v5.2 is an analytical overlay — it adds explanability and cross-validation. The active risk model is v0.8.5. Contract
v52layer.noteEnd: values are unchanged by the v5.2 layer.

limitations.item1Text: v0.8.5 trained on 1,417 cases and 861 vendors (~302K training contracts) with a per-vendor cap of 100. Salud and Agricultura sectors still contribute a disproportionate share of positive training data due to the IMSS, Segalmex, and DICONSA/LICONSA ecosystems.
limitations.item2Text: Cases with few contracts (La Estafa Maestra: 10, Grupo Higa: 3, Oceanografia: 2) have lower detection rates. The model requires sufficient contract volume to detect patterns reliably.
limitations.item3Text: Structure A (2002-2010) has only 0.1% RFC coverage. Z-scores and risk estimates are less reliable for this period. Network analysis requires 2010+ data for meaningful results.
limitations.item4Text: The PU correction (c=0.300) reflects the heterogeneity of expanded GT — many newly added vendors have ambiguous patterns. The SCAR assumption is structurally violated: known cases are high-profile scandals, not a random sample of all corruption.
limitations.item5Text: The co_bid_rate coefficient was regularized to exactly 0.0 in both the global model and all 12 per-sector sub-models. Co-bidding patterns do not discriminate in our current ground truth.
limitations.item6Text: A high risk score indicates a statistical anomaly consistent with documented corruption patterns. It does not constitute proof of wrongdoing. Risk scores are intended to prioritize investigation, not determine guilt.

v33section.p1End: with 8 base factors aligned to the IMF Corruption Risk Index methodology. Each factor contributed a percentage of the total score, with interaction bonuses for correlated factor pairs. Scores were preserved in the
v33section.bonusFactors: Additional bonus factors: Co-bidding +5%, Price Hypothesis +5%, Industry Mismatch +3%, Institution Risk +3%. 5 interaction pairs with up to +15% bonus. Score capped at 1.0.

dataSources.p1End: , the Mexican government's official electronic procurement system. The data spans 4 different structures with varying column counts and quality levels.
dataSources.rejectEnd: Contracts exceeding 100B MXN are excluded as data entry errors (decimal point mistakes in original COMPRANET data).
dataSources.flagEnd: Contracts between 10B and 100B MXN are included but marked for manual review.
dataSources.context: Context: Mexico's entire federal budget is ~8T MXN annually. A 100B contract would be 1.25% of the national budget.

references.footer: Risk scores are statistical indicators with confidence intervals. A high score indicates similarity to documented corruption patterns -- it does not constitute proof of wrongdoing.

hero.headline: Model Transparency
hero.subtitle: How we score {{n}} Mexican federal procurement contracts for corruption risk — the variables, the math, and the auditable trail.

tiers.kicker: HOW TO INTERPRET FINDINGS · CONFIDENCE TIERS
tiers.intro: Not all RUBLI findings carry equal certainty. Every claim — whether in a risk score, an ARIA flag, or a story — belongs to one of three tiers. The tier determines the language you should use when citing or reporting it.
tiers.tierIBody: An independent authority — SAT, SFP, or a documented judicial process — has formally established fraud, simulated operations, or a procurement irregularity.
tiers.tierIIBody: Algorithmic risk signal plus at least one independent external source pointing in the same direction. Neither source alone is conclusive; together they warrant priority investigation.
tiers.tierIIEx1: · Critical risk score + EFOS Presunto match
tiers.tierIIIBodyStart: RUBLI's algorithm detected procurement characteristics that statistically resemble known corruption cases. No external source corroborates the flag.
tiers.tierIIIBodyStrong: Most RUBLI findings are Tier III.
tiers.tierIIISay: "statistical pattern consistent with" · "flagged for investigation" · "behavioral anomaly"
tiers.criticalRuleBody: Never use Tier I language ("confirmed," "proven") for Tier III findings. The RUBLI risk score is not a verdict — it is a triage signal. A score of 0.90 means strong resemblance to documented corruption patterns, not a 90% probability of guilt. Shell company behavioral pattern flags (P2 pattern) have a 0.7% SAT confirmation rate — the other 99.3% are unconfirmed algorithmic detections.

limitations.intro: These limitations are inherent to the data sources, modeling approach, and legal constraints of the platform. Understanding them is essential to interpreting risk scores correctly.
limitations.interpretationBody: Risk scores are statistical indicators — not verdicts. A high score means strong similarity to documented corruption patterns. A low score does not certify a contract is clean. Use scores for investigation triage only; follow-up with primary sources to establish facts.

dataLabels.riskMeaningCritical: Very high similarity to known corruption patterns
dataLabels.riskMeaningHigh: High similarity to known corruption patterns
dataLabels.riskMeaningMedium: Moderate similarity to known corruption patterns
dataLabels.riskMeaningLow: Low similarity to known corruption patterns
dataLabels.ariaModelCoefficients: Dot matrix showing model coefficient values by feature
dataLabels.ariaRiskFactorWeights: Dot matrix showing risk factor weights in the v3.3 model

limitationsTable.row2_f: Add more labeled ground truth cases
limitationsTable.row3_i: Small-shell EFOS vendors still challenging to detect
limitationsTable.row5_i: Bid rotation & cover bidding not in score
limitationsTable.row6_i: Future data unavailable; 1.9M records already deleted
limitationsTable.row8_f: By design — model informs, not concludes
limitationsTable.row10_i: New fraud patterns may be undetected
limitationsTable.row12_f: Better labeled data from SAT, ASF
limitationsTable.row13_i: Vendor aggregates use full history; mitigated by v0.8.5 temporal split
limitationsTable.row14_i: HR=11.01% (within OECD 2–15% range); intercept -2.616 floor applied
limitationsTable.row15_i: External-flags +0.20 IPS boost guarantees GT vendors enter T1; T2 is the actual discovery surface
